record.field: Coder is a self-hosted platform for cloud development environments (CDEs) that provisions remote, ready-to-code workspaces on your own infrastructure.

Coder is a self-hosted platform for cloud development environments (CDEs) that provisions remote, ready-to-code workspaces on your own infrastructure.

Coder lets platform teams define developer workspaces as Terraform infrastructure-as-code, then run them on EC2 VMs, Kubernetes pods or Docker containers under their own governance. Developers connect from VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, the web or the CLI over a secure WireGuard tunnel, so source code and credentials never leave the company's environment. It is widely regarded as the self-hosted alternative to GitHub Codespaces, and now also governs autonomous AI coding agents that execute on your infrastructure rather than in unmanaged sandboxes.

Key features
  • Workspaces defined as code via Terraform, deployable on Kubernetes, cloud VMs (EC2 and others) or Docker
  • Secure WireGuard tunneling so code and secrets stay inside your network and air-gapped deployments are supported
  • Native IDE integrations for VS Code, JetBrains, web terminal and CLI/SSH access
  • Automatic shutdown of idle workspaces to control compute cost
  • Governed AI coding agents that run on your own infrastructure without exposing API keys in workspaces
  • Enterprise controls (access policies, audit logging, workspace proxies and branding) in the Premium edition
Best for

Platform engineering teams and enterprises that need standardized, reproducible developer environments with full control over infrastructure, security and compliance, including regulated or air-gapped settings and teams adopting governed AI coding agents.

services: coder: image: ghcr.io/coder/coder:v2.15.3 volumes: - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock group_add: - '988' depends_on: db: condition: service_healthy environment: - CODER_ACCESS_URL - CODER_HTTP_ADDRESS - CODER_PG_CONNECTION_URL=postgresql://${POSTGRES_USER}:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}@db/${POSTGRES_DB}?sslmode=disable container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}' restart: unless-stopped ports: - '{{ PORT_HTTP }}:7080' networks: - internal - agz-proxy-network db: image: postgres:17 environment: - POSTGRES_PASSWORD - POSTGRES_USER - POSTGRES_DB healthcheck: test: - CMD-SHELL - pg_isready -U ${POSTGRES_USER} -d ${POSTGRES_DB} interval: 5s timeout: 5s retries: 5 volumes: - ./db_coder_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_db' restart: unless-stopped networks: - internal networks: internal: driver: bridge agz-proxy-network: external: true

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR. # Si POSTGRES_USER/POSTGRES_DB vienen vacíos (la imagen usa 'postgres' por defecto), # caemos a 'postgres': con $POSTGRES_USER vacío pg_dump intentaría el rol "root" del SO # y fallaría con «role "root" does not exist». docker compose exec -T db sh -c 'pg_dump -U "${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}" -d "${POSTGRES_DB:-${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}}" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura (mismo default a 'postgres') docker compose up -d db for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T db sh -c 'pg_isready -U "${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T db sh -c 'pg_restore -U "${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}" -d "${POSTGRES_DB:-${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}}" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T db sh -c "psql -U \${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres} -d \${POSTGRES_DB:-postgres} -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
